Browse Topics Search

Dispelling a myth

Betsy Hammond The Oregonian July/August 2001
Education

The article discusses how Oregon's suburban schools are not as exceptional as often claimed. Using analysis that accounts for family backgrounds, the research identifies schools that underperform despite privileged demographics.

Oregon education officials have emphatically touted our state's public schools as among the very best in the world, and parents in most Portland suburbs say they moved to their particular part of our metro area for the great schools. However, our newspaper used computer-assisted reporting, supplemented with a lot of in-the-field reporting, to establish that Oregon's suburban schools are overwhelmingly mediocre - and to name which schools have done the least to help students reach their academic potential.

Our series, 'Why Not the Best?' (http://www.oregonlive.com/special/oregonian/education) drew hundreds of calls and e-mails from outraged readers. The Oregonian used SPSS to run regression analyses on student test scores, using family background statistics to 'level the playing field' among schools and see how each local school performs, given the students who come through their doors. The Oregonian added some new twists to its analysis, focusing not on finding schools in impoverished neighborhoods that do well, but on privileged neighborhood schools that were coasting or failing.

We anchored our findings to national data, providing a data-grounded reality check for parents who were hearing relentless drumbeats of boosterism about Oregon schools being 'world-class.'
